data.title: APi Group targets $10B revenue by 2028 while expanding M&A activity and margin growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: APi Group Corporation (APG) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Russell A. Becker highlighted "record second quarter results" with net revenues increasing by 15% and organic growth over 8%. He emphasized "consistent margin expansion in Safety Services, a growing inspection, service and monitoring business, a return to organic growth in Specialty Services, record backlog in both segments; and finally, an acceleration of accretive bolt-on M&A activity."
  • Becker introduced the new long-term "10/16/60+ shareholder value creation framework" with targets of "$10 billion plus in net revenues by 2028, 16% plus adjusted EBITDA margin by 2028, 60% plus of our revenues from inspection, service and monitoring over the long term and $3 billion-plus of cumulative adjusted free cash flow through 2028."
  • The company completed six acquisitions in Q2 and seven year-to-date, including a second elevator business, and announced "several more opportunities under letter of intent."
  • Becker stated, "We remain on track to deploy approximately $250 million in accretive bolt-on M&A at attractive multiples this year."
  • CFO Glenn David Jackola reported, "Reported revenues for the 3 months ended June 30 were $2 billion, a 15% increase compared to $1.73 billion in the prior year period."
  • Jackola noted, "Adjusted EBITDA increased by 17.7% for the 3 months ended June 30, with adjusted EBITDA margin coming in at 13.7%, representing a 30 basis point increase compared to the prior year period."

Outlook

  • APi Group increased full year 2025 net revenue guidance to "$7.65 billion to $7.85 billion, up from $7.4 billion to $7.6 billion, representing organic growth in net revenues of 4% to 7% for the year."
  • Full year adjusted EBITDA guidance is now "$1.005 billion to $1.045 billion, up from $985 million to $1.035 billion, representing adjusted EBITDA growth of approximately 15% at the midpoint."
  • Q3 2025 guidance is for net revenues of "$1.985 billion to $2.035 billion," with organic revenue growth of 5% to 7%, and adjusted EBITDA of "$270 million to $280 million."
  • Jackola said, "Our increased full year revenue and EBITDA guidance is driven by updates to our business outlook, including the impact of closed M&A during the quarter, our second quarter overdelivery and our latest outlook for the rest of the year."

Financial Results

  • Net revenues for Q2 were $2 billion, up 15% year-over-year; organic growth was 8.3%.
  • Adjusted diluted earnings per share for Q2 was $0.39, an increase of $0.06 or 18.2% compared to the prior year period.
  • Safety Services revenues for Q2 were $1.36 billion (up 15.8%), with organic growth of 5.6%. Segment earnings margin rose to 17%, an 80 basis point increase.
  • Specialty Services organic revenues for Q2 were $629 million (up 13.3%), with gross margin at 18.1%, a 350 basis point decrease, and segment earnings margin at 11.3%, a 190 basis point decrease.
  • Adjusted free cash flow for the first half was $186 million, with a 40% conversion.
  • The company increased its revolving credit facility to $750 million, extended maturity to 2030, and ended the quarter with a net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio of approximately 2.2x.

Q&A

  • Timothy Michael Mulrooney, William Blair: Asked about revenue outperforming guidance and margin pressure in Specialty. Jackola responded, "We saw really strong contract and project activity across both of the segments during the second quarter... Rising material costs and the impact of weather did play a role on our margins in the quarter."
  • Andrew John Wittmann, Baird: Inquired about the nature of the guidance increase. Jackola explained, "You can think of our EBITDA raise as 1/3 of it driven by our Q2 over delivery, maybe 1/3 of it due to M&A in the quarter and maybe 1/3 of it due to an increase or an improvement in our second half business outlook."
  • Julian C.H. Mitchell, Barclays: Asked about Safety business growth rates and elevator market share. Jackola indicated, "We continue to target mid- to upper single-digit revenue growth in the service side of the business, low to mid-single digit on the project to get to that mid-single-digit, 5% to 6% revenue growth in the back half of the year."
  • Jonathan E. Tanwanteng, CJS Securities: Queried on elevator acquisition margin profile. Becker replied it is "really closer to fleet average... with the potential to go and improve."
  • Jasper James Bibb, Truist Securities: Sought color on Specialty projects pipeline and margin outlook. Jackola said, "I'd expect high single-digit organic revenue growth in the Specialty business in the third quarter."
  • Tomohiko Sano, JPMorgan: Asked about inspection business margin improvements and technology use. Jackola stated, "We continue to be able to capture low to mid-single-digit pricing in our inspection service and monitoring revenue streams... our expectation on all of our revenue streams is that we're going to continue to be able to expand margin into '26, '27 and '28."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on the sustainability of growth, margin pressures in Specialty Services, and M&A discipline, with a generally positive but probing tone, seeking specifics on outlook improvements and margin levers.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone, with Becker repeatedly expressing optimism about business momentum, stating, "We are confident in our leaders' ability to execute our strategy and deliver against our 2025 plan."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, current analyst sentiment is more positive, reflecting surprise at outperformance and raised guidance. Management's tone remains highly confident during both prepared remarks and Q&A, increasingly forward-looking.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Full year net revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance were both raised from the prior range, with organic growth targets increased from 2%-5% to 4%-7%.
  • Backlog reached a record high above $4 billion, compared to $3.5 billion in Q1.
  • Q2 saw a return to organic growth in Specialty Services, following a decline in Q1.
  • Margin expansion continued in Safety Services, while Specialty Services gross margin declined due to increased project starts and material costs.
  • M&A activity accelerated, with six acquisitions in Q2 versus targets articulated in Q1.
  • Management’s confidence and forward-looking focus increased compared to Q1, with more explicit long-term targets and strategic clarity.

Risks and Concerns

  • Rising material costs and weather were cited as key challenges, particularly impacting Specialty gross margins.
  • Management discussed potential impacts from tariffs and inflation, but indicated that mitigation strategies, such as contractual provisions and disciplined project selection, are in place.
  • Execution risks remain around integrating acquisitions and delivering on elevated backlog margins.
  • Labor availability and efficiency were mentioned as ongoing operational challenges, with technology and AI initiatives aimed at offsetting workforce constraints.

Final Takeaway

APi Group delivered record Q2 results with strong top line growth, improved margin performance in Safety Services, and a return to organic growth in Specialty Services. The company raised its full year revenue and EBITDA guidance, driven by robust backlog, accelerating M&A, and positive momentum in inspection, service, and monitoring businesses. Management reiterated its commitment to disciplined capital deployment and outlined new long-term growth targets, projecting $10 billion-plus net revenues and 16%+ adjusted EBITDA margin by 2028. The outlook remains positive, supported by a strong balance sheet, ongoing free cash flow generation, and a focused strategy to navigate macroeconomic uncertainties.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Commodity Roundup: Gold faces third weekly loss; industrial metals slide amid U.S. trade moves
data.description:
Gold prices rose on Friday but were on track for a third straight weekly loss as a stronger dollar crimped safe-haven demand, while industrial metals posted losses across the board this week as markets digested a combination of US trade moves with sluggish demand in Mainland China.

Strong economic data out of the US, alongside the US-EU trade deal announcement, saw the US dollar index rise to a 2-month high just shy of the 100.0 mark, putting downward pressure on gold.

Following the US Fed’s decision to hold rates in July, Fitch's BMI said its macro team continues to expect a cumulative 50bps of cuts before the end of the year, which should support bullion as a non-yielding asset.

With further tariff announcements also likely, it expects gold prices to remain elevated and maintain the 2025 forecast of $3,100/oz.

Meanwhile, benchmark copper prices on the London Metal Exchange slumped 2.3% to $9,698/tonne after reports emerged that Chile – the world’s largest copper miner - was attempting to secure an exemption to Trump’s 50% tariff before its introduction on August 1.

Copper traded in New York plunged more than 20% on July 30 after the White Houseannounced that the 50% tariff would not apply to imports of refined copper.

"Other base metals also performed poorly this week, with aluminium and nickel prices falling 1.9% and 3.5% w-o-w to close at $2,601/tonne and $15,021/tonne, respectively, as Chinese demandcontinued to disappoint," the report said.

Ferrous metals also ended the week lower after China’s official manufacturing PMI registered the fourth consecutive month in contraction, falling to 49.3 in July from 49.7 in June. 

Elsewhere, soybean oil has outperformed the rest of the vegetable oils complex over he first half of 2025, the research by BMI said. 

On a year-to-date basis up to July 29, CBOT-listed second-month soybean oil futures posted a 42% increase.

data.title: GFL raises 2025 adjusted EBITDA target to $1.95B-$1.975B as pricing and margin expansion accelerate
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: GFL Environmental Inc. (GFL) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Patrick Dovigi reported "broad-based outperformance with which we started the year, driving results ahead of expectations despite multiple external headwinds" and highlighted adjusted EBITDA margins of 34.7% in solid waste, described as "the highest Q2 in our company's history." Dovigi emphasized that both pricing and volume were higher than expected, and announced an increase in pricing guidance to "over 5.5% pricing for the year."
  • Dovigi indicated the company completed three small tuck-in acquisitions in the quarter and expects to close three more imminently, maintaining confidence in meeting or exceeding M&A capital deployment targets for 2025. He stated the timing positions GFL for "a larger rollover amount into 2026, positioning us for yet another year of exceptional growth."
  • CFO Luke Pelosi stated, "Consolidated revenue for the quarter of $1.675 billion was 9.5% ahead of the prior year pro forma for divestitures." Pelosi further highlighted, “pricing of 5.8%, 30 basis points ahead of plan,” with volume growth accelerating in both geographies, and reiterated that positive volume trends were achieved despite macro headwinds. Pelosi also said, “Second quarter adjusted EBITDA margin was 30.7%, 230 basis points higher than the prior year and 60 basis points ahead of our guide.”

Outlook

  • GFL raised its full-year 2025 revenue guidance to "approximately $6.55 billion to $6.75 billion," up from the original range of $6.5 billion to $6.55 billion, adjusting for FX. Pelosi noted, “Our updated guidance would have been $6.625 billion to $6.65 billion on a constant currency basis, representing a 1.7% increase over our original guidance.”
  • The company increased its adjusted EBITDA target for the year to "$1.95 million to $1.975 million," a $25 million increase at current FX rates or a $50 million increase over the original guide on a constant currency basis. Pelosi added, “At the midpoint, year-over-year margin expansion increases to 120 basis points, an incremental 20 basis points over our original guidance.”
  • For Q3 2025, GFL expects consolidated revenue of approximately "$1.69 billion to $1.695 billion" and adjusted EBITDA of "$525 million," implying an adjusted EBITDA margin of about 31%. Q3 adjusted free cash flow is expected at "$175 million."

Financial Results

  • For Q2 2025, consolidated revenue was reported at "$1.675 billion," with adjusted EBITDA margin at "30.7%, 230 basis points higher than the prior year." Adjusted free cash flow stood at "$137 million," attributed to EBITDA outperformance and CapEx timing, while $190 million year-to-date investment in working capital is expected to mostly reverse by year-end.
  • Pelosi remarked, “The updated guidance assumes pricing of 5.5% to 5.75%, volume of positive 25 to 75 basis points and net M&A contribution of 40 to 50 basis points.”
  • Net CapEx for the year is forecasted at approximately "$750 million," with incremental investments expected to be largely offset by reduced cash taxes from recent U.S. tax legislation.

Q&A

  • Sabahat Khan, RBC Capital, asked about options for the GIP business and the $24 million monetization. Dovigi replied, “We’re looking to conclude that process over the next 2 to 3 weeks…we’re on the 5-yard line, we’re down to 2 final bidders…you’ll see a rebase of our equity significantly higher.”
  • Stephanie Moore, Jefferies, inquired about the M&A pipeline and market expansion. Dovigi responded, “Visibility is very good…continued focus is on densifying the existing markets where we have underutilized post-collection assets.”
  • Patrick Brown, Raymond James, asked for clarification on volume growth and guidance. Pelosi explained EPR is providing a tailwind to consolidated volumes, stating, “EPR is contributing about $20 million, $25 million of the global volume number.”
  • Kevin Chiang, CIBC, questioned upside to EPR EBITDA. Pelosi clarified, “What we’re seeing in the current year is not those incremental opportunities…this is really…ramping to $130 million of EBITDA faster than we otherwise would have.”
  • Konark Gupta, Scotiabank, probed revenue and EBITDA guidance drivers. Pelosi detailed, “Incremental M&A completed…$70 million to $80 million of incremental M&A contribution…$75 million of FX headwinds…bringing up pricing 50 bps, bringing up volume roughly 50 bps.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly positive, with questions focused on guidance clarity, M&A pipeline, volume performance, and margin drivers, seeking specific breakdowns and outlook.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone during prepared remarks and Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing "broad-based outperformance," “robust pipeline,” and “very good visibility.” Quotes such as “we’re feeling very good about it” and “we are increasing our pricing guidance” reflected high confidence.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remains confident but is more assertive in raising guidance and margin expectations, while analysts’ tone is similarly constructive but more focused on forward-looking guidance details.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for revenue and adjusted EBITDA was increased from Q1, with the range for both metrics raised and margin expansion targets updated.
  • Margin performance and pricing guidance are now above prior quarter levels, while M&A activity remained robust as stated in both transcripts.
  • Analyst questions shifted from clarifying strategy and margin drivers in Q1 to a greater focus on the composition and sustainability of volume growth, M&A contributions, and FX impacts in Q2.
  • Management demonstrated higher conviction in margin expansion and pricing power, and analysts responded with more granular inquiries on growth drivers and risk factors.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "macro headwinds impacting construction-oriented volumes and industrial demand," as well as FX and commodity price movements as external challenges.
  • Dovigi noted, “We believe the current tariff environment and broader economic uncertainty are limiting activity levels of many of our industrial customers.”
  • Pelosi indicated that “commodity prices, surcharges and contribution from FX were all headwinds to plan.”
  • Labor turnover was addressed as improving, but management continues to monitor wage pressures and labor stability.

Final Takeaway

GFL Environmental delivered record-setting Q2 results, raising full-year revenue and adjusted EBITDA targets while achieving historic margin performance. Management underscored strong pricing execution, robust M&A activity, and resilient volume growth, positioning the company for continued industry-leading expansion despite persistent macroeconomic and FX headwinds. The updated outlook reflects increased confidence in both organic and inorganic growth levers, with an emphasis on disciplined capital deployment and margin expansion for the remainder of 2025 and into 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Climb Global Solutions outlines operational efficiency gains and strategic M&A focus for 2025
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Climb Global Solutions (CLMB) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Dale Richard Foster stated the company experienced "another strong quarter with material increases across all of our key financial metrics." He highlighted double-digit organic growth, new vendor partnerships, and expanded market share in both the U.S. and Europe. Foster noted that the acquisition of Douglas Stewart Software (DSS) contributed incremental and seasonal strength, especially as demand ramped up for education customers.
  • Foster emphasized a disciplined approach to vendor evaluation, sharing that "in Q2 alone, we evaluated 50 potential vendor partnerships and move forward with just 4 of them." He announced a partnership with Ignite, enabling access to its cloud-native platform, and an exclusive distribution agreement with IGEL for the U.K. and Ireland, building on prior regional partnerships.
  • The appointment of Vishal Pushpa as Chief Information Officer was announced, bringing over two decades of IT leadership and experience in large-scale technology transformations. Carlos Rodrigues was promoted to President of North America, responsible for sales acceleration and further market expansion.
  • Foster shared that "with our ERP system now fully in place, we're beginning to realize the benefits of improved operational efficiency and scalability, positioning us to drive stronger operating leverage as we grow." He also mentioned active evaluation of strategic M&A opportunities in North America and overseas to expand capabilities and geographic reach.
  • CFO Matthew Sullivan stated "gross billings in Q2 of 2025 increased 39% to $500.6 million compared to $359.8 million in the year ago quarter." He reported distribution segment gross billings growing 40% and solutions segment gross billings rising 19%. Sullivan noted net sales increased 73% to $159.3 million, driven by organic growth and DSS acquisition contribution.

Outlook

  • Foster indicated continued focus on building momentum from the first half of the year, executing on strategic priorities, and leveraging the fully implemented ERP system for greater scalability. He also referenced ongoing evaluation of M&A opportunities aligned with long-term growth objectives.
  • Sullivan reiterated "we're continuing to explore strategic acquisitions that align with our high-performance culture and strengthen our ability to meet evolving customer needs."
  • No explicit forward guidance metrics or EPS/revenue projections were provided in the transcript.

Financial Results

  • Sullivan reported gross profit grew 42% to $26.3 million, with gross profit as a percentage of gross billings increasing to 5.3%. SG&A expenses rose to $16.4 million, with $900,000 attributed to DSS, while SG&A as a percentage of gross billings declined to 3.3%.
  • Net income increased 74% to $6 million, or $1.30 per diluted share. Adjusted net income rose 68% to $6.4 million, or $1.39 per diluted share. Adjusted EBITDA climbed 64% to $11.4 million, with an effective margin of 43.3%.
  • The company ended the quarter with $28.6 million in cash and $500,000 in outstanding debt. A quarterly dividend of $0.17 per share was declared.

Q&A

  • Vincent Alexander Colicchio, Barrington Research: Asked if security and data center continued to lead growth or if it was broadening. Foster replied "those are our top 2 and security being the stronger... that's going to be our leader for quite some time."
  • Colicchio: Inquired about top 20 vendor performance versus overall business. Foster explained there was more movement among vendors ranked 10-20, with new entrants performing better, and highlighted Darktrace's expected impact in the second half of the year.
  • Colicchio: Asked about large deals influencing the quarter. Foster noted a significant VAST Data order was pulled into Q2 from Q3, contributing to results but affirmed organic growth remained strong without it.
  • Colicchio: Questioned synergies from DSS acquisition. Foster confirmed integration and ERP implementation, with teams now quoting and processing DSS product lines.
  • Unidentified Analyst: Sought clarity on gross margin trends. Sullivan stated "we continue to project it to be in that 5%, 5.1%" with current quarter's higher margin driven by lumpy transactions. Foster added that margin expansion would depend on growing the solutions team and adding more services.
  • Unidentified Analyst: Asked about SG&A trends. Sullivan expects 3.3% of gross billings to be consistent moving forward.
  • Unidentified Analyst: Probed on international tariffs and currency. Sullivan reported no significant impact from tariffs and ongoing efforts to optimize currency hedging strategies.
  • Unidentified Analyst: Discussed growth headroom beyond $2 billion in gross billings. Sullivan described significant market opportunity, stating "it's a lot of headroom that we can go before we'd be... disruptive to them."
  • Unidentified Analyst: Asked about acquisition strategy and valuations. Foster said current targets are smaller, cash-funded, strategically aligned services companies, with larger deals possible in 2026 or 2027; acquisition multiples start at 7 to 9 times.
  • Colicchio: Sought views on economic headwinds and business robustness. Foster noted no signs of headwinds, citing small market share and a strong vendor pipeline, but disclosed the loss of Citrix in Ireland, which is expected to be offset by other products.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed positive sentiment, congratulating management and highlighting the lack of negative aspects in the results. Questions focused on sustainability of growth, margin trends, and market opportunity.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing execution and market headroom. Phrases such as "we're continuing to explore strategic acquisitions" and "we look forward to continuing our partnership" reflected optimism.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analyst sentiment remained positive, with continuity in themes of growth, disciplined vendor selection, and market expansion.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company maintained double-digit organic growth, with incremental benefit from the DSS acquisition in both quarters. The previous quarter emphasized ERP system implementation, while the current quarter highlighted its completion and efficiency gains.
  • Strategic focus remained on disciplined vendor selection and expanding solutions and services. The previous quarter saw the addition of a board member, while the current quarter included new leadership appointments and exclusive partnership agreements.
  • Management's confidence was consistent, underscoring growth momentum and operational improvements. Analysts continued to focus on growth drivers and margin trends, with similar positive sentiment in both periods.
  • Key metrics such as gross billings, net sales, and profitability showed sequential improvement.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management mentioned the lumpy nature of some large deals, such as the VAST Data order being pulled forward, which may affect quarter-to-quarter comparisons.
  • Exposure to currency fluctuation was discussed, with ongoing efforts to improve hedging strategies. Tariffs were not seen as a current risk.
  • The loss of Citrix in Ireland was acknowledged, but management expressed confidence in filling the gap with other vendors and products.

Final Takeaway

Climb Global Solutions closed the quarter with strong organic and acquisition-driven growth, highlighted by efficient ERP implementation, new leadership, and expansion of exclusive vendor partnerships. Management remains focused on executing strategic priorities, exploring targeted M&A, and leveraging operational scalability for continued growth in 2025, while emphasizing the company’s significant market headroom and resilience against sector headwinds.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Laureate outlines 2025 revenue guidance increase to $1.615B–$1.630B while advancing campus expansion in Mexico and Peru
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Laureate Education, Inc. (LAUR) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Eilif Serck-Hanssen, President and CEO, reported "strong execution across the board for the second quarter and the first half of the year." He highlighted a 7% year-to-date increase in new enrollments and a 6% rise in total enrollments versus the prior year, driving 9% growth in revenue on a timing adjusted and constant currency basis.
  • Serck-Hanssen announced, "we are raising full year 2025 outlook at the midpoint by $55 million for revenue and $16 million for adjusted EBITDA." He confirmed that two new campuses in Monterrey, Mexico and Lima's Ate District are on track to open in September, with two additional projects underway for opening late next year or early 2027, underlining the company's commitment to growth in both markets.
  • The CEO emphasized resilience in both Mexico and Peru, citing macroeconomic stability and strong demand for quality higher education as tailwinds.
  • Serck-Hanssen stated, "we remain confident in the growing demand for quality, affordable higher education across both markets and our ability to capitalize on those growth opportunities given our leading brands, strong value proposition and industry-leading digital capabilities."
  • Richard M. Buskirk, Chief Financial Officer, said, "Revenue in the seasonally strong second quarter was $524 million, and adjusted EBITDA was $214 million. Both metrics were ahead of the guidance provided three months ago, aided by favorable currency rates and timing of expenses." Buskirk detailed that net income for the quarter was $97 million, with earnings per share of $0.65 on a reported basis and adjusted EPS of $0.79.

Outlook

  • Management has increased full year 2025 revenue guidance to a range of $1.615 billion to $1.630 billion, reflecting growth of 3% to 4% on an as-reported basis, and 6% to 7% on an organic constant currency basis. Adjusted EBITDA is now expected in the range of $489 million to $496 million, reflecting growth of 9% to 10% on an as-reported basis.
  • Total enrollments are projected in the range of 491,000 to 495,000 students for the year, which is 4% to 5% growth versus 2024.
  • For Q3 2025, revenue guidance is $375 million to $379 million, and adjusted EBITDA is forecast at $78 million to $82 million, incorporating a $7 million unfavorable intra-year academic calendar timing impact.
  • Buskirk noted, "The approximately 150 basis points of margin expansion at the midpoint of our guidance will be driven by operating leverage from revenue growth, our campus consolidations in Mexico and lower corporate expenses."

Financial Results

  • The company reported second quarter revenue of $524 million and adjusted EBITDA of $214 million. Net income for the quarter was $97 million, with EPS of $0.65 and adjusted EPS of $0.79.
  • Revenue for the second quarter was up 8% year-over-year on an organic constant currency basis and adjusted for the academic calendar shift, while adjusted EBITDA grew 13%.
  • Mexico's new enrollments increased 6% through June, with total enrollments up 7%. Revenue in Mexico for Q2 increased 9% year-over-year, adjusted EBITDA rose by 19%, and year-to-date growth in Mexico's revenue was 10%.
  • Peru's primary intake concluded with total enrollment growth of 6%, revenue increased 7% year-over-year, and adjusted EBITDA was up 9% for the quarter. Year-to-date, Peru's revenue was up 7% and adjusted EBITDA increased 10%, with margins expanding by 111 basis points.
  • Laureate ended June with $135 million in cash and $116 million in gross debt, resulting in a net cash position of $19 million. The company repurchased $71 million of common stock through June as part of its $100 million buyback program.

Q&A

  • Ryan (BMO Capital Markets) asked about early indications for Mexico's fall intake pipeline and revenue upside. Eilif Serck-Hanssen responded, "We are about halfway through the main intake...very, very encouraged by the strength of the working adult students. And as I said, well underway now with the main intake, which is primarily young students...so far so good." On revenue upside, Buskirk explained, "The $20 million of outperformance versus the high end of the range was primarily associated with FX...then we did have $2 million of operational outperformance that was a bit of a carryforward from a strong enrollment intake."
  • Lucas Dai Nagano (Morgan Stanley) inquired about online learning trends in Peru and campus expansion plans. Serck-Hanssen explained that online programs in Peru are "growing double digits" and are seeing "very strong interest by the working adult students," similar to prior trends in Mexico. For new campuses, he confirmed openings in Monterrey and Lima's Ate, with two more under development. He also detailed the capital expenditure algorithm, expecting CapEx to rise to 5% of revenues to support ongoing campus launches.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly positive, focusing on enrollment trends, digital expansion, and revenue drivers, with follow-up questions seeking detail rather than expressing concern.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone throughout both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently referencing operational strength and ongoing momentum. Serck-Hanssen described being "very, very encouraged" and Buskirk noted outperformance "primarily associated with FX."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the sentiment remained confident but included more explicit optimism regarding growth opportunities, particularly as new campus projects move forward and digital platforms scale.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company raised its full year revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance by $55 million and $16 million, respectively, compared to the prior quarter's guidance.
  • Strategic emphasis shifted further toward expansion, with a clear focus on new campus openings and digital learning growth in Peru.
  • Analysts continued to focus on enrollment trends and capital allocation, but the Q2 call highlighted greater interest in digital program adoption and campus rollout timelines.
  • Key financial metrics, including revenue, adjusted EBITDA, cash position, and net cash, all improved compared to Q1.
  • Management's confidence in operational execution and market opportunity was more pronounced, with less discussion of macroeconomic headwinds and more emphasis on favorable trends.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged ongoing geopolitical and macroeconomic uncertainties in both Mexico and Peru but expressed confidence in the resilience of their business model.
  • The timing of academic calendar shifts and FX volatility were highlighted as factors impacting revenue recognition and guidance.
  • Analysts probed for details on intake trends and revenue drivers but did not raise new material concerns.

Final Takeaway

Laureate Education's management highlighted strong year-to-date performance, robust enrollment growth, and increased revenue guidance for 2025. The company emphasized its strategic expansion with two new campuses opening in September and plans for further development in both Mexico and Peru. Digital program growth, particularly in Peru, was described as rapidly accelerating, mirroring earlier success in Mexico. Management remains confident in both the operating environment and the company's ability to drive sustainable growth, supported by disciplined execution and a strong balance sheet.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oculis upsized loan facility to access up to CHF 100 million
data.description:
• Oculis Holding (NASDAQ:OCS) has amended its loan facility with funds and accounts managed by BlackRock.
  • The upsized structure will provide CHF 75.0 million in borrowing capacity, which may be increased to up to CHF 100.0 million.
  • The loan comprises three tranches of CHF 25.0 million each, as well as an additional loan of up to CHF 25.0 million, which may be made available on mutually agreed terms.
  • No amounts were drawn at signing.

data.title: Myers Industries signals strategic shift with MTS review and targets $20M in cost savings by year-end 2025
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Myers Industries (MYE) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Aaron M. Schapper, President and CEO, stated that "second quarter review was lower year-over-year. We achieved strong growth in certain applications, notably in industrial, where demand for our military products remain healthy. However, we did encounter demand headwinds in most other end markets, primarily in vehicle and automotive aftermarket, resulting in lower sales across both segments." Schapper emphasized a positive outlook for the second half of the year, citing a "substantial backlog, primarily in industrial markets, especially military as well as infrastructure projects."
  • Schapper announced three key actions under the Focused Transformation program: a strategic review of the Myers Tire Supply (MTS) business, consolidation of rotational molding production capacity by idling two of nine facilities (projected annual savings of at least $3 million), and reaffirmation of the $20 million SG&A cost reduction target, noting "we have line of sight to $18 million, well on our way to our $20 million goal."
  • Schapper further explained, "with today's announcements, we have made a large significant step to simplify and focus our portfolio with the MTS strategic review. We are also rationalizing our operations with the consolidation of rotational molding production."
  • Daniel W. Hoehn, Interim CFO, reported, "Second quarter net sales were $209.6 million, down 4.8% from last year. Revenue was lower in both segments...The adjusted gross margin fell 220 basis points to 33.9% due to the lower volume, product sales mix and lower pricing, primarily in the Distribution segment."
  • Hoehn highlighted, "we reduced adjusted SG&A expenses 5%, keeping them essentially flat as a percentage of sales as we are beginning to see results from our Focused Transformation initiatives."

Outlook

  • The company reconfirmed its 2025 market outlook, maintaining expectations set in the previous quarter. Hoehn stated, "we still see both risks and opportunities for the businesses, and we'll continue to monitor end market conditions for impacts from tariffs or other factors that may influence demand trends."
  • Myers now expects "sales of our military products to exceed $40 million for the full year of 2025."
  • The industrial market is projected for moderate growth, infrastructure spending is expected to remain strong, and the vehicle end market is forecasted to be down due to economic uncertainty and tariff impacts.
  • Automotive aftermarket distribution is expected to be slightly down, with stabilization efforts ongoing through cost structure improvements and digital initiatives.

Financial Results

  • Net sales for the quarter were $209.6 million.
  • Adjusted gross margin was reported at 33.9%.
  • Adjusted operating income reached $22.8 million, with a margin of 10.9% of sales.
  • Free cash flow for the quarter was $24.7 million, up $22.7 million sequentially and $14.8 million from the prior year.
  • At quarter end, the company reported $239.7 million in revolving credit facility availability and $41.3 million in cash on hand.
  • Debt was reduced by $13 million during the quarter, with a reported net leverage ratio of 2.8x.
  • Share repurchases totaled $0.5 million in Q2, with $8.5 million remaining under the current authorization.

Q&A

  • Christian Zyla, KeyCorp: Asked about the rationale behind the MTS strategic review and the timeline that led to the decision. Schapper replied, "the MTS piece has been -- it's been an internal topic of discussion for some time...the first 6 months was really just to take the proper time to do the evaluation."
  • Zyla inquired about the size and relevance of the backlog. Hoehn responded, "for that business and as we grow in military, we'll see more backlog in those specific areas. I think for the other businesses, it depends, and we see a lot more kind of book and bill as we go."
  • Zyla also questioned the strength of free cash flow. Hoehn attributed it to timing and indicated that "historically, we've had a little bit more cash flow in the back half," and that recent performance reflects what the business should achieve.
  • Carolina Jolly, Gabelli & Company: Asked about confidence in a rebound for seed boxes and integration of the Signature acquisition. Schapper cited customer demand and seasonality for seed boxes and noted that Signature "brings a unique set of operational talents that we're actually able to really utilize across more of our material handling footprint."
  • William Dezellem, Tieton Capital Management: Asked about the impact of tariffs and idling of rotational molding plants. Hoehn explained export customers delayed purchases due to tariff uncertainty and Schapper added, "we do have some resolution, so we hope to not have those kind of disconnects in the next quarter."
  • Dezellem also probed on the fate of idled plants. Schapper clarified, "right now, that capacity is not needed," and both are leased facilities.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, with repeated focus on backlog visibility and strategic changes. The tone was positive when discussing transformation steps, but probing around demand, tariffs, and operational changes suggested underlying concern.
  • Management's prepared remarks were confident, as Schapper stated being "pleased with the progress we are making against our Focused Transformation objectives and confident in our team's ability to deliver improved financial results."
  • During Q&A, management maintained a steady but careful tone, occasionally referencing hope for future stability, as when Schapper said, "we hope to not have those kind of disconnects in the next quarter."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was more assertive regarding transformation actions, while analysts' skepticism persisted around execution and market challenges.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The Q2 call introduced decisive strategic actions: the MTS business review and consolidation of manufacturing facilities, which were not present in Q1.
  • Cost reduction targets were reaffirmed and progress quantified, advancing from early-stage plans discussed last quarter.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on the impact of tariffs, free cash flow, and market demand, but Q2 saw increased attention on backlog and the tangible progress of transformation.
  • Financial performance showed a sequential increase in free cash flow and continued cost discipline, while revenue declined year-over-year in Q2 versus flat performance in Q1.
  • Management's tone moved from establishing transformation plans to executing specific portfolio and operational changes.

Risks and Concerns

  • Demand headwinds in vehicle and automotive aftermarket segments remain a challenge.
  • Tariff uncertainty continues to affect timing and volume of export sales, though management anticipates some near-term resolution.
  • The strategic review of MTS introduces uncertainty regarding the future of that business and its impact on the overall portfolio.
  • Two rotational molding plants are being idled, reflecting lower current demand; management stated the capacity could be revisited if needed in the future.

Final Takeaway

Myers Industries is accelerating its Focused Transformation strategy with a strategic review of its Myers Tire Supply business and operational consolidation, targeting $20 million in cost reductions by the end of 2025. Despite near-term demand softness in several markets and ongoing tariff uncertainty, the company is maintaining a positive outlook for the second half of the year, supported by a strong backlog in military and infrastructure applications. Management underscores confidence in achieving financial targets and further portfolio simplification, while continuing to focus on disciplined capital allocation and shareholder returns.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Quanta Services raises 2025 outlook to $27.4B–$27.9B revenue and $10.28–$10.88 EPS while advancing strategic acquisitions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Quanta Services (PWR) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Earl C. Austin, President and CEO, reported "strong double-digit growth in revenue, adjusted EBITDA and adjusted earnings per share, along with record backlog of $35.8 billion and a number of other record financial metrics." The company announced the acquisition of Dynamic Systems, described as "a premier turnkey mechanical, plumbing and process infrastructure solutions provider" that will expand Quanta's addressable market and capabilities, particularly in technology, manufacturing, and other load center markets. Austin stated that Dynamic Systems "brings an exceptional management team and a premier craft-skilled workforce that complement Quanta's culture."
  • Austin noted a strategic investment in Bell Lumber and Pole Company, enhancing Quanta's core utility infrastructure equipment portfolio. He emphasized that "Quanta's core strategy is built on the foundation of craft-skilled labor, execution certainty, investment discipline and clear strategic rationale."
  • Austin highlighted the increasing demand for power and infrastructure: "Utilities across the United States are experiencing and forecasting meaningful increases in power demand, which is being driven by the adoption of new technologies and related infrastructure, including data centers and artificial intelligence..."
  • CFO Jayshree S. Desai stated, "This morning, we reported strong second quarter results, including revenues of $6.8 billion, net income attributable to common stock of $229 million or $1.52 per diluted share, adjusted diluted earnings per share of $2.48 and adjusted EBITDA of $669 million." She reported cash flow from operations of $296 million and free cash flow of $170 million. Desai added, "Our second quarter performance was ahead of our expectations across most financial metrics and similar to the first quarter, is allowing us to increase our full year expectations before considering the contribution from acquisitions."
  • Desai explained, "We are currently evaluating refinancing alternatives to increase our post-transaction liquidity profile, ensuring we can continue to support operations and opportunistically invest capital."

Outlook

  • Desai raised full-year 2025 guidance: "Revenues are now expected to range between $27.4 billion and $27.9 billion, adjusted EBITDA between $2.76 billion and $2.89 billion and adjusted EPS between $10.28 and $10.88."
  • She noted confidence in demand: "While we recognize the variability in the regulatory environment, demand for Quanta's differentiated portfolio of self-performed craft labor solutions remains strong."
  • The increased 2025 financial expectations were attributed to solid second quarter performance and expected contributions from recent acquisitions and investments.

Financial Results

  • The company reported revenues of $6.8 billion, net income attributable to common stock of $229 million, $1.52 per diluted share, adjusted diluted earnings per share of $2.48, and adjusted EBITDA of $669 million.
  • Cash flow from operations reached $296 million, and free cash flow was $170 million for the quarter.
  • Quanta achieved a record backlog of $35.8 billion.
  • Management stated that second quarter performance was "ahead of our expectations across most financial metrics."

Q&A

  • Andrew Kaplowitz, Citi Research, asked about sequential backlog growth and long-term EPS growth potential despite political and regulatory noise. Austin responded, "I do believe the things that we're doing today set us up for the future, '26, '27, '28 and '29. What we've done is, we basically built platforms against TAMs that compound."
  • Sherif El-Sabbahy, Bank of America, inquired about backlog and bidding process. Austin explained, "It's longer in nature. We're talking about '26, '27, '28 type time frames of growth and CAGR growth and EPS growth and EBITDA growth."
  • Atidrip Modak, Goldman Sachs, focused on the Dynamic Systems acquisition. Austin said the acquisition was customer-driven and aligned with Quanta's strategy, adding, "Yes, we will continue to look at great family businesses that add value to our strategy and our shareholders."
  • Philip Shen, ROTH Capital, questioned the outlook for renewables given policy changes. Austin replied, "I still believe, it's really about the lowest cost, the speed to market that the renewables provide, we are seeing pull-ins on LNTPs."
  • Jamie Cook, Truist Securities, asked about internal preparations for short-term slowdowns and revenue synergies from Dynamic. Austin described labor flexibility and portfolio derisking: "We do move labor quite a bit across segments across TAMs."
  • Jayshree Desai confirmed Dynamic Systems had about $1.8 billion of backlog, which would be updated in Q3.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on growth sustainability, strategic acquisitions, labor flexibility, and regulatory risks. The tone was largely constructive with some pressing for detail on renewables, backlog, and M&A rationale.
  • Management maintained a confident, forward-looking tone, emphasizing strong demand, portfolio resilience, and the strategic value of recent acquisitions. Austin stated, "We intend to remain investment grade."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained confident, with even more emphasis on growth opportunities from acquisitions and increasing addressable markets. Analyst sentiment was similarly constructive but included more questions about the impact and integration of new acquisitions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and adjusted EPS was raised again this quarter, following a similar upward revision in Q1, but now includes the expected contribution from Dynamic Systems and Bell Lumber.
  • Management placed greater emphasis on platform acquisitions and cross-segment labor flexibility this quarter, describing Dynamic Systems as a new growth platform.
  • Record backlog increased from $35.3 billion in Q1 to $35.8 billion in Q2.
  • Analysts' questions shifted toward integration of recent acquisitions and the evolving renewables landscape, compared to Q1's focus on core market growth and transmission opportunities.
  • Management continued to stress portfolio derisking and long-term growth drivers, with explicit references to multi-year growth outlooks extending through 2029.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged variability in the regulatory environment and the potential impact of policy changes on renewables but expressed confidence in customer safe harbor strategies and multiyear pipelines.
  • Analysts raised concerns about policy headwinds, safe harbor rules, and the pace of renewables, but management indicated that "demand side from every one of our customers we keep hearing is as strong as ever."
  • The company is monitoring liquidity and leverage post-acquisition, with Desai stating that Quanta aims to maintain leverage between 1.5x and 2x and expects to be below 2x by year-end.

Final Takeaway

Quanta Services management highlighted strong financial performance, record backlog, and the successful integration of strategic acquisitions such as Dynamic Systems and Bell Lumber as key drivers of increased 2025 guidance. The company emphasized its platform approach, labor flexibility, and solution-based model, positioning itself for sustained long-term growth across expanding addressable markets, while maintaining a prudent balance sheet and active risk management amid regulatory and policy uncertainties.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oil States outlines $88M–$93M EBITDA guidance and margin growth as offshore mix rises
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Oil States International (OIS) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Cynthia B. Taylor, President and CEO, highlighted the company's performance "achieving the midpoint of our guided EBITDA range for the second quarter of 2025 due to our product and service mix." She reported that "72% of our consolidated revenues were generated from offshore and international projects, up significantly sequentially and year-over-year." Taylor described a strategic shift toward international project-driven revenues and ongoing optimization of U.S. land operations amid lower domestic activity and competitive market dynamics.
  • Taylor stated, "Driven by strong demand across our international and offshore markets, our Offshore/Manufactured Products segment delivered strong performance. Revenues increased 15% sequentially, while adjusted segment EBITDA rose 18%. Backlog increased to $363 million again, allowing us to achieve our highest level since September 2015."
  • The company exited three additional land-based facilities and reduced its U.S. land-focused workforce in response to declining U.S. land drilling and completion activity. Taylor emphasized, "Our deleveraging efforts should unlock additional equity value to our stockholders as we have approached net debt 0 and pay off our convertible senior notes at their maturity in April 2026."
  • Lloyd A. Hajdik, Executive VP and CFO, stated, "During the second quarter, we generated revenues of $165 million and adjusted consolidated EBITDA of $21 million. Net income totaled $3 million or $0.05 per share, which included facility exit, severance and other charges and credits totaling $3 million. Our adjusted net income totaled $5 million or $0.09 per share after excluding these charges and credits."

Outlook

  • Taylor confirmed, "We are maintaining our full year EBITDA guidance in a range between $88 million to $93 million. However, our revenue guidance needs to be updated for the streamlining of our U.S. land operations, which will reduce our full year revenue range to $685 million to $700 million."
  • For the third quarter, Taylor provided guidance for "revenues in the range of $165 million to $170 million and EBITDA of $21 million to $23 million."
  • The company expects "cash flow from operations, which are still expected to be in a range of $65 million to $75 million for the full year."

Financial Results

  • Hajdik reported, "Our Offshore/Manufactured Products segment generated revenues of $107 million and adjusted segment EBITDA of $21 million in the second quarter. Adjusted segment EBITDA margin was 20% in the second quarter compared to 19% in the first quarter."
  • The Completion and Production Services segment delivered "revenues of $29 million and adjusted segment EBITDA of $8 million in the second quarter. Adjusted segment EBITDA margin was 28%, benefiting from facility and equipment sale gains in the second quarter compared to 25% in the first quarter."
  • Downhole Technologies segment produced "revenues of $29 million and $1 million of adjusted segment EBITDA in the second quarter."
  • The company generated $15 million of cash flow from operations, used $10 million for CapEx, received $3 million from asset sales, repurchased $7 million in common stock, and purchased $15 million of convertible senior notes.

Q&A

  • James Michael Rollyson, Raymond James: Asked for color on offshore project timing and the impact of uncertainty. Taylor responded that project deferrals seen elsewhere are "discretionary type investments" and that Oil States' business is "much more weighted to production infrastructure associated with these large fields that have already been drilled and discovered," emphasizing multi-decade developments and robust bookings outlook.
  • Rollyson asked about tariff impacts. Taylor said, "we just don't anticipate a material impact from the tariff situation, given our variety of global supply sourcing... The one area that we'll probably have modest cost increases is actually in the Downhole...the perforating side of the business, which, as you know, is rather small for us."
  • Rollyson asked for CapEx guidance clarity. Hajdik answered, "we're going to guide to CapEx about $30 million because we are a little higher in the second quarter for the completion of the Batam and some specific built riser equipment for customer contracts." Taylor added, "what's new is this low-impact workover riser...equipment built for future revenue streams."
  • Patrick John Ouellette, Stifel: Queried normalized revenue mix. Taylor stated, "of the 28% current land base mix, about half of that comes from our Downhole Technologies segment."
  • Ouellette asked about margin progression. Hajdik projected, "Upper 20s to low 30s." Taylor said, "noticeable almost a doubling of our EBITDA margins by these actions."
  • John Matthew Daniel, Daniel Energy Partners: Asked about U.S. land-based business mix. Taylor said, "everything we have left is really focused on completion activity, 0 drilling."
  • Charles P. Minervino, Susquehanna: Questioned full year guidance step-up. Taylor explained, "It is going to be led by our Offshore/Manufactured Products Segment. And most of it is based on backlog build."
  • Stephen David Gengaro, Stifel: Asked about offshore order flow. Taylor responded, "we are looking at a book-to-bill north of 1 throughout the balance of this year and do have optimism as we go into 2026."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts probed guidance sustainability, margin improvements, and the competitive landscape, with a neutral-to-positive tone, especially as Taylor and Hajdik addressed concerns with specifics and confidence.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing actions such as "our bookings outlook remains robust," "we anticipate continued strength," and "we are committed to optimizing our operations." Taylor's language was assertive regarding backlog, cost structure, and future outlook.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts sustained a constructive, confident, and well-articulated exchange, with the main shift being more emphasis on offshore and international strengths and clarity regarding U.S. land exit impacts.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management reaffirmed full-year EBITDA guidance but lowered revenue guidance (from $700 million–$735 million to $685 million–$700 million) due to further U.S. land operation streamlining.
  • Margin outlook improved, with a focus on higher offshore/international mix and cost reductions, compared to the previous quarter's emphasis on broad-based diversification and initial land optimization.
  • Backlog hit a new decade high, and the share of offshore/international revenues rose from 1Q's strong levels to 72% in 2Q.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from cost and tariff impacts to sustainability of offshore momentum and the effects of U.S. land retrenchment on long-term margins.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing geopolitical instability, lower crude oil prices, and fluctuating U.S. trade policies as macro risks.
  • Taylor noted, "U.S. land drilling and completion activity declined significantly during the period," and that further rationalization may continue.
  • Tariff-related cost increases are expected to be modest and concentrated in the Downhole Technologies segment.
  • Management's mitigation measures include further facility exits, workforce reductions, and global sourcing flexibility.

Final Takeaway

Oil States International emphasized its strategic pivot toward offshore and international markets, delivering strong backlog growth and improved margins, while continuing to optimize its cost structure and streamline U.S. land operations. The company maintained its full-year EBITDA outlook but adjusted revenue guidance to reflect business mix changes. Management remained confident in sustained cash flow generation, future bookings, and the ability to unlock equity value through disciplined capital allocation, all underpinned by a robust offshore project pipeline and ongoing deleveraging efforts.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: AMZN, FIG, and more
data.description:
Stock futures edged down Friday morning as investor sentiment took a hit from concerns over the economic impact of higher tariffs, with market participants also awaiting the closely watched U.S. payrolls report due later in the day.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Figma (NYSE:FIG) +18% – Shares extended gains after a stunning NYSE debut on Thursday, where they soared 250% intraday, briefly topping $105. The design software firm priced its IPO at $33 per share, above the expected range, and saw its market value surpass $50B shortly after listing. The explosive rally marked one of the most dramatic IPO openings in recent memory.

  • Reddit (NYSE:RDDT) +14% – Shares rallied after the company delivered a double beat in Q2 and issued strong Q3 guidance. Daily active users rose 21% Y/Y to 110.4M, while revenue surged 78% to $500M, driven by 84% growth in ad sales. Q3 revenue is expected at $535M–$545M above the consensus of $472.7M with adjusted EBITDA of $185M–$195M (consensus $159.5M).

Biggest stock losers

  • Coinbase Global (NASDAQ:COIN) -9% – Shares plunged after Q2 adjusted EPS of $0.12 fell far short of the $1.49 consensus, sharply down from $1.93 in the prior quarter and $1.10 a year ago. Total net revenue of $1.42B also missed estimates of $1.59B, declining from $1.96B in Q1 but up from $1.38B a year earlier. Looking ahead, Coinbase expects Q3 subscription and services revenue of $665M–$745M (midpoint $705M vs. $742M consensus), transaction expenses to grow in the mid-teens as a percentage of net revenue, tech and G&A costs of $800M–$850M, and sales and marketing spend of $190M–$290M.

  • Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) -7% – Shares declined even after the company topped Q2 expectations on revenue, EPS, and operating income, as its Q3 operating income guidance midpoint came in below consensus. Amazon expects Q3 revenue of $174.0B–$179.5B (midpoint $176.3B vs. $173.3B consensus) and operating income of $15.5B–$20.5B (midpoint $18.0B vs. $19.4B consensus). CEO Andy Jassy highlighted constraints in AI growth due to limited electricity and chip supply, noting capital expenditures of $31.4 billion in Q2 will likely persist for the remainder of the year as Amazon invests further in chips, data centers, and power infrastructure.
